headline: The U.S. government promised to protect you from forever chemicals. This is what actually happened.

stats.label: Americans drink tap water contaminated with PFAS
stats.source: EWG analysis of EPA UCMR3 data

stats.label: of Americans have PFAS in their bloodstream
stats.source: CDC National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES)

stats.label: — Biden-era limit for PFOA/PFOS in drinking water, set April 2024

healthEffects: Weakened immune response (including reduced vaccine effectiveness)
Developmental harm in fetuses and infants
whatIsPfas: PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) are a family of roughly 12,000 synthetic chemicals first developed in the 1940s. They are used in nonstick cookware, waterproof clothing, firefighting foam (AFFF), food packaging, and hundreds of industrial processes. The carbon-fluorine bond — the strongest single bond in organic chemistry — means nothing in nature breaks these compounds down. They persist in soil, water, and living tissue indefinitely. That is why they are called 'forever chemicals.'
whyItMatters: PFAS bioaccumulate: they build up in human tissue over time. Studies link them to multiple cancers, immune suppression, thyroid disruption, and developmental harm. Children are particularly vulnerable. The chemicals spread through contaminated water systems, food packaging, and indoor air. Most Americans are exposed before they even know it.
